tasks.title: Deciding When to Claim Social Security
tasks.what: Choosing your Social Security start age — 62, your full retirement age (FRA), or 70 — is one of the most consequential financial decisions you'll make in retirement.
tasks.why: Claiming early permanently reduces your benefit by up to 30%; delaying past FRA increases it 8% per year. A wrong decision can cost tens of thousands of dollars over your lifetime.
steps: Find your full retirement age (FRA): born 1943–1954 = 66; born 1960 or later = 67; years in between are graduated at 2-month increments.
Look up your estimated benefit at each age by creating a my Social Security account at ssa.gov/myaccount.
Calculate your break-even age: divide the total dollars gained by delaying by the monthly difference in benefit. Most break-even points fall between ages 78–82.
Factor in your health, other income sources, and whether you're still working — earning more than $24,480/year (2026) before FRA triggers a temporary benefit reduction.
If married, model spousal benefits: a spouse can claim up to 50% of your FRA benefit, so the higher earner delaying to 70 often maximizes household lifetime income.
Consider survivor benefits: when one spouse dies, the survivor keeps only the larger of the two benefits, making the higher earner's delay even more valuable.
Run scenarios using the Social Security Administration's online calculator or a tool like Open Social Security (opensocialsecurity.com) for a free optimization report.
Document your decision and the reasoning so you can revisit it if your health or finances change before you file.

terms.def: The age at which you receive 100% of your Social Security benefit, currently 67 for anyone born in 1960 or later.
The 8% annual increase in your benefit for each year you delay claiming past your FRA, up to age 70.
The age at which the cumulative total of a larger delayed benefit overtakes the cumulative total of a smaller early benefit.
A rule that requires married individuals who file for one Social Security benefit to file for all benefits they're eligible for simultaneously, limiting certain strategies.

tasks.contacts: Social Security Administration • 800-772-1213 • ssa.gov • Open Social Security calculator: opensocialsecurity.com
mistakes: Claiming at 62 simply because you're eligible, without modeling lifetime totals against your life expectancy.
Assuming both spouses should claim at the same age — coordinated filing strategies almost always outperform mirrored ones.
Forgetting that Social Security benefits are subject to federal income tax if your combined income exceeds $25,000 (single) or $32,000 (married).
Not accounting for the earnings test if you plan to work part-time before reaching FRA.

tasks.title: Applying for Social Security Benefits
tasks.what: The formal process of filing your Social Security claim so benefits start at your chosen date.
tasks.why: Benefits don't start automatically — you must apply, and the process takes time. Missing your target start month can mean losing benefits you're entitled to.
steps: Apply exactly 4 months before you want benefits to begin — SSA recommends applying 3–4 months ahead, and your first payment arrives about one month after your start date.
Apply online at ssa.gov/apply (fastest method, takes about 15 minutes for most applicants) or call 800-772-1213 to schedule an in-person appointment.
Gather required documents before applying: Social Security card or number, birth certificate, most recent W-2 or self-employment tax return, bank account and routing number for direct deposit.
If applying for spousal benefits, also have your spouse's Social Security number and your marriage certificate ready.
Choose your payment start date carefully — you can request retroactive benefits up to 6 months back (if you're past FRA), but retroactive months are paid at a lower effective rate.
Set up or confirm direct deposit during the application to avoid delays — SSA no longer mails paper checks to new recipients.
After filing, watch for a confirmation letter within 2–3 weeks; if you don't receive one, follow up by calling 800-772-1213.
Review your first benefit statement carefully and report any discrepancies to SSA immediately.

terms.def: A lump-sum payment for up to 6 months of past benefits available to applicants who are already past FRA when they file, effectively backdating the claim.
The required electronic transfer of Social Security payments to a bank or credit union account; SSA no longer issues paper checks to new beneficiaries.

tasks.contacts: Social Security Administration • 800-772-1213 • ssa.gov/apply • Local SSA office locator: ssa.gov/locator
mistakes: Waiting until your desired start month to apply — processing takes time and you may miss your target payment date.
Not having documents ready, which delays your application and can push back your first payment.
Forgetting to apply for Medicare Part B at the same time if you're approaching 65, since they're often coordinated at SSA.

tasks.title: Understanding Spousal and Survivor Benefits
tasks.what: Social Security provides additional benefits for spouses and widows/widowers that can significantly increase household income if claimed strategically.
tasks.why: Many married retirees leave thousands of dollars unclaimed each year by not understanding how spousal and survivor benefits interact with their own record.
steps: Understand the spousal benefit: a current spouse can receive up to 50% of the other spouse's FRA benefit — not their actual benefit — as long as the primary earner has filed.
Know that spousal benefits do not earn delayed retirement credits past FRA — there is no advantage to a spouse waiting past their own FRA to claim a spousal benefit.
If you have your own work record, SSA pays your own benefit first; the spousal top-up is only the difference between your benefit and 50% of your spouse's FRA benefit.
For divorced spouses: if your marriage lasted at least 10 years and you're currently unmarried, you may claim a spousal benefit on your ex's record without affecting their benefit.

Understand survivor benefits: a widow or widower can claim as early as age 60 (or 50 if disabled) and receives up to 100% of the deceased spouse's benefit — including any delayed retirement credits earned.
Plan the survivor strategy now: the higher earner delaying to 70 creates the largest possible survivor benefit for the lower-earning spouse.
Report a spouse's death to SSA promptly by calling 800-772-1213 — funeral homes often do this, but confirm it was done.
If receiving survivor benefits, reconsider whether to switch to your own record at 70 if it would be larger — or vice versa.

terms.def: A Social Security benefit worth up to 50% of a spouse's FRA benefit amount, available to current spouses and qualifying ex-spouses.
A benefit paid to a widow, widower, or qualifying dependent after a Social Security recipient dies, worth up to 100% of the deceased's benefit including any earned credits.

A rule requiring that anyone filing for Social Security before or at FRA must file for all benefits simultaneously, preventing the old strategy of filing for spousal benefits while delaying one's own.

mistakes: Assuming a spouse must wait until 70 to maximize a spousal benefit — spousal benefits don't grow past FRA.
Failing to claim a survivor benefit while letting a personal benefit grow, or vice versa — both strategies are valid depending on amounts and ages.
A divorced spouse not realizing they may qualify for benefits on an ex's record, especially if they have a limited work history.

tasks.title: Enrolling in Medicare on Time
tasks.what: Understanding Medicare's enrollment windows to avoid late-enrollment penalties that last for life.
tasks.why: Missing your Initial Enrollment Period triggers a 10% Part B premium penalty for every 12-month period you were eligible but didn't enroll — permanently.
steps: Know your Initial Enrollment Period (IEP): a 7-month window starting 3 months before the month you turn 65, including your birthday month, and ending 3 months after.
If you're already receiving Social Security when you turn 65, you're automatically enrolled in Medicare Parts A and B — watch your mail for your red, white, and blue card.
If not auto-enrolled, apply for Medicare at ssa.gov/medicare or call 800-772-1213 during your IEP.
Enroll in Part A (hospital insurance) first — it's premium-free for most people with 40+ quarters of work history.

Decide on Part B (outpatient coverage, $202.90/month standard premium in 2026) — you can delay Part B penalty-free only if covered by a qualifying employer group health plan through active employment.
Do not confuse COBRA or retiree health coverage with qualifying coverage — neither exempts you from the Part B penalty clock.
Sign up for Part D (prescription drug coverage) during your IEP or within 63 days of losing other creditable drug coverage to avoid the Part D late enrollment penalty (1% per month).
If you miss your IEP without a qualifying exemption, your next chance is the General Enrollment Period: January 1–March 31 each year, with coverage starting July 1.

terms.def: The 7-month window around your 65th birthday during which you can first enroll in Medicare without penalty.
A permanent 10% premium surcharge added for each full 12-month period you were eligible for Part B but did not enroll without a qualifying exemption.
Drug coverage from an employer or union that is at least as good as Medicare Part D, which exempts you from the Part D late enrollment penalty while enrolled.
A Medicare enrollment window outside the IEP triggered by specific life events, such as losing employer coverage due to retirement.

tasks.contacts: Medicare • 800-633-4227 (800-MEDICARE) • medicare.gov • Social Security Administration (for enrollment) • 800-772-1213 • ssa.gov/medicare
mistakes: Thinking retiree health insurance or COBRA coverage counts as creditable coverage that delays the Part B penalty clock — it does not.
Missing the 7-month IEP and waiting for the General Enrollment Period, which adds months of no coverage and triggers a permanent premium penalty.
Not enrolling in Part D because you take no prescriptions now — you still owe the penalty when you eventually do need drug coverage.

script.intro: When calling Medicare (1-800-633-4227) or SSA (1-800-772-1213) to enroll:
lines: "I'm calling to enroll in Medicare. I'm turning 65 on [date] and I'm currently [still working with employer coverage / retired / on COBRA]. I want to make sure I enroll in the right parts and don't miss any deadlines."

"Can you confirm my Initial Enrollment Period dates? I want to enroll in Part A and Part B. I also need to understand my Part D options. Do I need to sign up for Part D now, or is my current drug coverage considered creditable?"
"I'm also trying to decide between a Medigap supplement and a Medicare Advantage plan. Can you point me to resources that compare plans available in my zip code?"
script.note: Call SSA (not Medicare) to actually enroll. Medicare's 800 number is for questions about coverage. Have your Social Security number, current insurance card, and a list of your doctors and prescriptions ready.

tasks.title: Choosing Between Medicare Supplement and Medicare Advantage
tasks.what: Deciding whether to pair Original Medicare with a Medigap supplement plan or to enroll in an all-in-one Medicare Advantage plan instead.
tasks.why: This choice affects your out-of-pocket costs, which doctors you can see, and your flexibility in getting specialized care — and switching later can be difficult or impossible.
steps: Understand the two paths: Original Medicare (Parts A + B) with a Medigap supplement and Part D drug plan, versus Medicare Advantage (Part C), which replaces Original Medicare with a private plan.

Compare Medigap benefits: Plan G (most popular in 2026) covers nearly all out-of-pocket costs after the Part B deductible ($283 in 2026); premiums average $100–$200/month depending on age and location.
With Medigap, you can see any provider that accepts Medicare nationwide — no referrals needed, no networks — making it preferable for frequent travelers and those with complex medical needs.
Compare Medicare Advantage: typically $0 or low monthly premiums, but you pay copays and coinsurance at the time of service, and most plans use provider networks (HMO or PPO).
Check whether your current doctors and any preferred hospitals are in-network before selecting a Medicare Advantage plan at medicare.gov/plan-compare.
Look at Medicare Advantage out-of-pocket maximums (capped at $8,850 in-network for 2026) — this is your worst-case annual cost.
Understand the switching risk: you can switch from Medigap to Medicare Advantage anytime, but going back to Medigap may require medical underwriting (and you could be denied) in most states.
During your initial Medigap Open Enrollment (the 6 months starting when you're 65 and enrolled in Part B), you have guaranteed issue rights — no health questions asked.

terms.def: Private insurance sold to fill the gaps in Original Medicare coverage, such as deductibles, copays, and coinsurance.
An alternative to Original Medicare offered by private insurers that bundles Parts A, B, and usually D into one plan, often with a network of providers.
A right during specific enrollment periods that requires insurers to sell you a Medigap policy regardless of your health status or pre-existing conditions.
The most you'll pay for covered services in a plan year under Medicare Advantage; Original Medicare with Medigap effectively has no cap without a supplement.

tasks.contacts: Medicare Plan Finder • medicare.gov/plan-compare • State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP) • shiphelp.org • 800-633-4227
mistakes: Choosing Medicare Advantage for the low premium without fully understanding network restrictions and potential high out-of-pocket costs for serious illness.
Enrolling in Medigap outside of guaranteed issue periods without realizing you can be denied based on health history.
Not comparing Part D drug plans annually during Open Enrollment (October 15–December 7) — formularies and premiums change every year.

tasks.title: Bridging Health Insurance Before Age 65
tasks.what: Finding health coverage for the gap between leaving employer insurance and Medicare eligibility at 65.
tasks.why: Going uninsured before 65 is a significant financial risk — a single hospitalization without coverage can cost $30,000 or more, and short-term plans may not cover pre-existing conditions.
steps: Calculate how many months you need to bridge — coverage must be continuous until your Medicare start date.
Option 1 — COBRA: elect within 60 days of losing employer coverage; you pay the full premium (often $600–$1,800/month for a family) plus a 2% administrative fee; coverage lasts up to 18 months.

Option 2 — Spouse's employer plan: if your spouse is still working, joining their plan is often the least expensive option; do this within 30 days of losing your own coverage to qualify for a special enrollment period.

Option 3 — ACA Marketplace plan: losing job-based coverage is a qualifying life event; shop at healthcare.gov within 60 days. If your income is under 400% of the federal poverty level (~$62,600 for a single person in 2026), you may qualify for premium tax credits.
Option 4 — Retiree health plan: some employers offer retiree coverage; ask HR before your last day, as enrollment windows are often very short.
Avoid short-term health plans as a primary strategy — they routinely exclude pre-existing conditions and are not ACA-compliant.
If you have an HSA, use it to pay COBRA or Marketplace premiums tax-free — HSA funds can cover premiums while receiving unemployment benefits.
Set a calendar reminder 7 months before your 65th birthday to start your Medicare enrollment process so coverage transitions seamlessly.

terms.def: A federal law that allows you to continue your employer's group health coverage for up to 18 months after leaving a job, at your own expense plus an administrative fee.
The Health Insurance Marketplace created by the Affordable Care Act where individuals can purchase ACA-compliant health insurance, often with premium tax credits based on income.
A window outside the annual Open Enrollment Period during which you can sign up for Marketplace or employer coverage due to a qualifying life event, such as losing other coverage.

tasks.contacts: ACA Marketplace • healthcare.gov • 800-318-2596 • COBRA administrator (contact your former HR) • SHIP counselors for Medicare transition help • shiphelp.org
mistakes: Missing the 60-day COBRA election window or the 60-day Marketplace SEP window — both have hard deadlines.
Assuming a retiree health plan automatically continues; many require active opt-in within days of leaving employment.
Underestimating the ACA premium tax credit — early retirees with modest income often qualify for substantial subsidies that make Marketplace plans more affordable than COBRA.

tasks.title: Taking Required Minimum Distributions (RMDs)
tasks.what: Understanding the IRS rules that require you to withdraw a minimum amount from traditional retirement accounts each year starting at age 73.
tasks.why: Failing to take your full RMD triggers a 25% excise tax on the amount you should have withdrawn — one of the steepest penalties in the tax code.
steps: Know which accounts require RMDs: traditional IRAs, SEP IRAs, SIMPLE IRAs, 401(k)s, 403(b)s, and most other employer-sponsored retirement plans. Roth IRAs do NOT have RMDs during the owner's lifetime (as of 2024 law).

Your first RMD is due by April 1 of the year after you turn 73; all subsequent RMDs are due by December 31 of each year. Taking two RMDs in one year (first + second) can push you into a higher tax bracket.

Calculate your RMD each year: divide your account balance as of December 31 of the prior year by your IRS Uniform Lifetime Table life expectancy factor (available at irs.gov/retirement-plans/plan-participant-employee/required-minimum-distributions-rmds).
If you have multiple traditional IRAs, calculate the RMD for each account separately but you can withdraw the total from any one or combination of your IRAs.
For 401(k)s, each account must satisfy its own RMD separately — you cannot aggregate them like IRAs.

If you're still working at 73 and participating in your current employer's 401(k), you can delay RMDs from that specific plan until you retire (the Still Working Exception) — but not from IRAs or old employer plans.
Set up automatic annual distributions with your custodian if you want to avoid missing the deadline — most major brokerages (Fidelity, Schwab, Vanguard) offer this service.

Consider a Qualified Charitable Distribution (QCD): if you're 70½ or older, you can donate up to $108,000/year (2026) directly from your IRA to a qualified charity, satisfying the RMD and excluding that amount from taxable income.

terms.def: The minimum amount the IRS requires you to withdraw annually from tax-deferred retirement accounts starting at age 73.
An IRS table used to calculate RMDs by dividing the prior year's account balance by a life expectancy factor corresponding to your age.
A direct transfer of up to $108,000 (2026) from an IRA to a qualified charity that counts toward your RMD but is excluded from your taxable income.
A rule allowing employees who are still working at 73 to delay RMDs from their current employer's retirement plan until they actually retire.

tasks.contacts: IRS RMD information • irs.gov/retirement-plans • 800-829-1040 • Your IRA or 401(k) custodian (Fidelity: 800-343-3548 • Schwab: 800-435-4000 • Vanguard: 800-662-7447)
mistakes: Taking the first RMD in April of the year after turning 73 without realizing a second RMD is still due by December 31 of the same year, causing a large tax bill.
Forgetting an old 401(k) at a former employer — those accounts also require RMDs and won't remind you.
Not knowing that inherited IRAs have separate and stricter RMD rules, including a 10-year depletion rule for most non-spouse beneficiaries.
Missing a QCD opportunity if you are charitably inclined — it's one of the best tax breaks available to retirees over 70½.

tasks.title: Creating a Withdrawal Strategy
tasks.what: Building a systematic plan for how much to withdraw each year and from which accounts to minimize taxes and maximize how long your money lasts.
tasks.why: Without a strategy, retirees often pull from accounts in a tax-inefficient order, pay more taxes than necessary, and risk depleting savings prematurely.
steps: Start with the 4% rule as a baseline: withdraw no more than 4% of your portfolio in year one, then adjust for inflation each year. On a $1,000,000 portfolio, that's $40,000/year.
Map your income sources: list Social Security, pensions, annuities, part-time income, and rental income before touching investment accounts to understand your actual shortfall.

Follow the standard tax-efficient withdrawal order: (1) RMDs first (you have no choice), (2) taxable brokerage accounts next (long-term capital gains taxed at 0%, 15%, or 20%), (3) traditional IRA/401(k) for additional needs, (4) Roth IRA last (tax-free growth preserved as long as possible).
Manage your tax bracket deliberately — in years when your taxable income is low, consider withdrawing more from traditional accounts or doing Roth conversions to fill up lower brackets.
Maintain 1–2 years of expenses in cash or short-term bonds (a "cash buffer") so you never have to sell investments during a market downturn to cover living expenses.
Review your withdrawal rate annually against portfolio performance; consider cutting spending by 10% in years following a major market decline (the "guardrails" approach).
Account for inflation: a 3% inflation rate means your $50,000 budget today needs ~$67,000 in 10 years and ~$90,000 in 20 years.
Work with a fee-only financial planner to run Monte Carlo projections that stress-test your plan across hundreds of market scenarios; look for a 90%+ success rate.

terms.def: A guideline suggesting that withdrawing 4% of your portfolio in the first year of retirement, then adjusting for inflation annually, has historically sustained a 30-year retirement.
The danger that poor market returns in the early years of retirement can permanently deplete a portfolio even if average long-term returns are adequate.
A strategy of drawing from taxable accounts first, then tax-deferred accounts, then Roth accounts last to minimize lifetime taxes.
A flexible withdrawal approach that adjusts spending up or down based on portfolio performance to reduce the risk of running out of money.

tasks.contacts: NAPFA fee-only planner search • napfa.org • 888-333-6659 • Garrett Planning Network • garrettplanningnetwork.com • Fidelity Retirement Income Planning • 800-343-3548
mistakes: Withdrawing from a Roth IRA early in retirement when taxable accounts with preferential capital gains rates should be depleted first.
Ignoring the tax impact of large RMDs on Social Security taxation, Medicare premium surcharges (IRMAA), and bracket creep.
Using a fixed 4% withdrawal without ever adjusting for actual market performance or spending changes.
Keeping too much in cash for comfort, causing the portfolio to underperform inflation over a 20–30-year retirement.

tasks.title: Making Your Pension Decision
tasks.what: Evaluating whether to take pension income as a monthly lifetime annuity or as a lump-sum payout, and which survivor option to select.
tasks.why: This is an irrevocable decision for most pension plans — once made, you cannot change it, so understanding all options before signing is critical.
steps: Request a pension election packet from your HR or plan administrator at least 3–6 months before your retirement date; review every payment option available.

Compare the lump sum to the annuity: ask your plan for the lump sum amount and divide the annual single-life annuity payment into it to find the "payout rate." If the rate exceeds what you could safely earn investing the lump sum (roughly 4–5%), the annuity may win.

Evaluate your health: if you have reason to believe your life expectancy is shorter than average, a lump sum rolled to an IRA may produce more total income; if you expect to live past 85, a lifetime annuity typically wins.

Understand survivor options: a "joint and 50% survivor" annuity pays you less monthly but continues at 50% to a surviving spouse; a "joint and 100% survivor" pays even less but continues the full amount. Never waive survivor benefits without your spouse's written, notarized consent.
If taking the lump sum, roll it directly to an IRA (trustee-to-trustee transfer) to avoid the mandatory 20% federal tax withholding on distributions paid directly to you.
Ask about Cost of Living Adjustments (COLAs): a pension with annual COLAs is far more valuable in an inflationary environment than one with a flat payment.

Factor in pension insurance: most private-sector pensions are insured by the PBGC up to $7,789.77/month (2026) for a 65-year-old; public pensions are typically backed by the state but may face funding issues.

Consider the "pension max" strategy only with professional guidance: taking the single-life annuity and buying life insurance to protect your spouse is sometimes advantageous but requires careful comparison of insurance costs and health qualifications.

terms.def: A pension payment option that pays the highest monthly amount but stops entirely at the retiree's death, with nothing continuing to a survivor.
A pension option with a lower monthly benefit that continues paying a portion (50%, 75%, or 100%) to a surviving spouse after the retiree's death.
A federal agency that insures most private-sector defined benefit pension plans, guaranteeing benefits up to a statutory maximum if a plan fails.
A direct rollover of retirement funds from one institution to another that avoids mandatory tax withholding and preserves the tax-deferred status of the funds.

tasks.contacts: Your HR or plan administrator (first contact) • PBGC • pbgc.gov • 800-400-7242 • Fee-only financial planner • napfa.org
mistakes: Choosing the single-life annuity to maximize monthly income without realizing a surviving spouse will receive nothing after your death.
Taking the lump sum as a direct distribution instead of a rollover, triggering immediate 20% withholding and a potential 10% early withdrawal penalty if under 59½.
Not asking about COLA provisions — a flat pension payment loses roughly half its purchasing power over 20 years at 3% inflation.

tasks.title: Understanding How Taxes Change in Retirement
tasks.what: Learning how retirement income — Social Security, RMDs, pensions, and investment withdrawals — is taxed differently than a paycheck.
tasks.why: Many retirees are surprised by their tax bills in retirement; understanding the rules lets you plan withdrawals to legally minimize what you owe.
steps: Understand the Social Security tax threshold: up to 85% of your Social Security benefit is taxable if your "combined income" (AGI + nontaxable interest + half of SS) exceeds $34,000 (single) or $44,000 (married). Below $25,000/$32,000, Social Security is tax-free.
Know that RMDs and traditional IRA/401(k) withdrawals are taxed as ordinary income — they can push you into a higher bracket and trigger Medicare IRMAA surcharges.

IRMAA (Income-Related Monthly Adjustment Amount) adds surcharges to Medicare Parts B and D premiums for higher-income retirees; in 2026, surcharges kick in above $109,000 (single) or $218,000 (married), and can add hundreds of dollars per month per person.
Long-term capital gains from taxable brokerage accounts are taxed at 0%, 15%, or 20% depending on income — far more favorable than ordinary income rates for most retirees.
Qualified dividends are taxed at the same rates as long-term capital gains — keep this in mind when building a tax-efficient portfolio.

State income taxes vary widely: some states (FL, TX, WA, and others) have no income tax; some exempt pension income; some partially exempt Social Security. Review your state's rules if you're considering relocating.
Pay quarterly estimated taxes if you expect to owe more than $1,000 in federal taxes for the year — retirement income typically doesn't have automatic withholding unless you request it.
Request voluntary withholding from Social Security (Form W-4V) and your pension/IRA custodian to simplify tax payments instead of making quarterly estimates.

terms.def: The IRS formula used to determine how much of your Social Security is taxable: Adjusted Gross Income + nontaxable interest + 50% of Social Security benefits.
Income-Related Monthly Adjustment Amount — a surcharge added to Medicare Parts B and D premiums for higher-income beneficiaries, based on income from 2 years prior.
Income taxed at regular federal tax rates (10%–37% in 2026), including wages, RMDs, traditional IRA withdrawals, and most pension income.
Quarterly IRS payments (due April 15, June 15, September 15, and January 15) required when income is not subject to automatic withholding.

tasks.contacts: IRS • irs.gov • 800-829-1040 • IRS Publication 554 (Tax Guide for Seniors) • irs.gov/pub/irs-pdf/p554.pdf • CPA or Enrolled Agent • naea.org
mistakes: Not withholding taxes from IRA distributions or Social Security and facing a large tax bill plus underpayment penalties in April.
Ignoring IRMAA — a single high-income year (such as a large Roth conversion or home sale) can trigger Medicare surcharges two years later.
Assuming retirement means a lower tax rate without modeling the combined impact of Social Security, RMDs, and other income sources.

tasks.title: Doing Roth Conversions in Early Retirement
tasks.what: Strategically moving money from traditional IRAs or 401(k)s to a Roth IRA during the lower-income years between retirement and RMD age.
tasks.why: The years between retiring and turning 73 are often your lowest-income years — a rare window to convert pre-tax savings to tax-free Roth money at a lower rate than you'll face when RMDs kick in.
steps: Identify your conversion window: the years after you stop working (and before Social Security, RMDs, or pension income fully phase in) when your taxable income is lowest.

Calculate how much to convert each year to "fill up" a tax bracket without crossing into a higher one. In 2026, the 22% bracket tops out at $105,700 taxable income for single filers; the 24% bracket goes to $201,775.

Remember that conversion income can also trigger Social Security taxation (if you're already collecting), IRMAA surcharges two years later, and loss of ACA premium tax credits if you're on a Marketplace plan.
Use tax software or a fee-only planner to model the conversion amount that maximizes long-term after-tax wealth — conversion math is counterintuitive without modeling.
Pay the conversion tax from taxable accounts, not from the converted IRA funds, to maximize the amount moved to Roth.
Understand the 5-year rule: each conversion has its own 5-year clock for penalty-free withdrawal of converted principal if you're under 59½; at 59½+ this rule doesn't apply to avoiding penalties.
Open a Roth IRA now if you don't have one — the account's 5-year holding period for tax-free earnings growth starts from when the account is first opened.
Revisit your conversion strategy every year as tax brackets, income, and tax law change — this is not a set-it-and-forget-it decision.

terms.def: The process of moving pre-tax money from a traditional IRA or 401(k) to a Roth IRA, paying income tax on the amount converted in the year of conversion.
The strategy of deliberately controlling the amount and timing of income to stay within a desired tax bracket and minimize total lifetime taxes.

A rule requiring that a Roth IRA account be open for at least 5 years before earnings can be withdrawn tax-free, and that each conversion amount be held for 5 years to avoid the 10% early withdrawal penalty (for those under 59½).

tasks.contacts: IRS Publication 590-A (IRA contributions) • irs.gov/pub/irs-pdf/p590a.pdf • Fee-only financial planner • napfa.org • CPA or tax advisor
mistakes: Converting too aggressively and pushing income into a bracket where the marginal tax rate exceeds the rate you'd have paid on RMDs later.
Paying conversion taxes from the converted IRA funds themselves — this shrinks the amount in Roth and may trigger a 10% early withdrawal penalty on the portion used for taxes if under 59½.
Forgetting that conversion income counts toward combined income for Social Security taxation and IRMAA calculations.
Not opening a Roth IRA until the conversion year — the 5-year clock for tax-free earnings growth starts from account opening, not from each conversion.

tasks.title: Updating Beneficiaries on All Accounts
tasks.what: Reviewing and updating the beneficiary designations on every retirement account, life insurance policy, and financial account to ensure assets pass to the right people.
tasks.why: Beneficiary designations override your will — an outdated designation can send assets to an ex-spouse, a deceased person, or your estate (triggering probate) regardless of what your will says.
steps: Compile a list of every account with a beneficiary designation: IRAs, 401(k)s, 403(b)s, life insurance policies, annuities, HSAs, and any accounts with a Transfer on Death (TOD) or Payable on Death (POD) designation.
Log in to each account or contact the custodian/insurer to review current beneficiary designations — many people haven't reviewed these since opening the account.
Update primary beneficiaries to reflect your current intentions. Name at least one contingent beneficiary in case your primary predeceases you.

For IRA beneficiaries, understand the rules: a spouse beneficiary has the most flexibility (can roll the IRA into their own); most non-spouse beneficiaries must deplete the inherited IRA within 10 years (the SECURE 2.0 rule).
Avoid naming your estate as beneficiary — doing so subjects the account to probate, removes the 10-year stretch option, and can cause delays and extra costs.
If you have minor children as intended beneficiaries, name a trust as beneficiary (with the trust drafted to handle the 10-year rule appropriately) rather than naming minors directly.
Keep copies of all completed beneficiary designation forms in a secure, accessible place — and tell your executor or trusted family member where to find them.
Review beneficiaries after every major life event: marriage, divorce, death of a beneficiary, or birth of a child or grandchild.

terms.def: The person or entity first in line to inherit account assets upon the owner's death.
A backup beneficiary who inherits if the primary beneficiary is deceased or unable to accept the inheritance.
A designation on a brokerage or bank account that passes the account directly to the named beneficiary at death, bypassing probate.
A provision under the SECURE Act requiring most non-spouse beneficiaries to fully deplete an inherited IRA or 401(k) within 10 years of the original owner's death.

tasks.contacts: Your IRA/401(k) custodian (each requires its own form) • Life insurance company • Your estate planning attorney for trust-as-beneficiary arrangements
mistakes: Naming a minor child directly as beneficiary — a court-appointed guardian will control the funds until the child reaches the age of majority, which may not align with your wishes.
Failing to name a contingent beneficiary, so if the primary beneficiary predeceases you the account may default to your estate and go through probate.
Not updating beneficiaries after a divorce — in many states, a former spouse remains the legal beneficiary unless you actively change the designation.

tasks.title: Reviewing Your Estate Plan
tasks.what: Confirming that your will, trust, powers of attorney, and healthcare directives are current, legally valid, and aligned with your current wishes and assets.
tasks.why: An outdated or incomplete estate plan can lead to family conflict, unintended asset distribution, court intervention, and a stressful, expensive probate process for the people you love.
steps: Locate all existing estate documents: will, revocable living trust (if any), durable financial power of attorney, healthcare power of attorney (healthcare proxy), and advance healthcare directive (living will).
Review your will: confirm it reflects your current wishes for asset distribution, names the right executor, and was signed in accordance with your state's witnessing and notarization requirements.

If you have a revocable living trust, confirm all major assets — real estate, brokerage accounts, bank accounts — are actually titled in the trust's name ("funding" the trust). An unfunded trust does not avoid probate.

Review your durable power of attorney: this document names someone to manage your finances if you become incapacitated. Confirm your agent is still willing, able, and trusted. Some financial institutions reject powers of attorney older than a few years.

Review your healthcare documents: your healthcare proxy names someone to make medical decisions if you cannot; your advance directive specifies your wishes for end-of-life care. Both should reflect your current values and be known to your healthcare agent.
Give copies of your healthcare directive to your primary care physician, any specialist you see regularly, and your local hospital system.
Consider whether a trust would benefit your estate: trusts avoid probate, provide privacy, and can include provisions for incapacity management that a will cannot.
Schedule a review with your estate planning attorney — a full review typically costs $500–$2,000 depending on complexity and is well worth it every 3–5 years or after any major life change.

terms.def: A legal arrangement where you transfer ownership of assets to a trust you control during your lifetime; it avoids probate at death and allows for incapacity management.
A legal document authorizing a person (your agent) to manage your financial and legal affairs, remaining valid even if you become mentally incapacitated.
A legal document, sometimes called a living will, that specifies your wishes for medical treatment if you are unable to communicate them yourself.
The court-supervised legal process of validating a will and distributing a deceased person's estate, which can be time-consuming, costly, and public.

tasks.contacts: Estate planning attorney (search ACTEC.org for a certified specialist) • National Academy of Elder Law Attorneys • naela.org • 800-522-6325
mistakes: Creating a living trust but failing to retitle assets into the trust, so everything still goes through probate anyway.
Using a generic online power of attorney form that major financial institutions refuse to honor — many institutions require their own forms or documents drafted by an attorney.
Not telling your healthcare agent where your directive is stored or what your wishes are, leaving them to guess in a crisis.

tasks.title: Building Structure and Purpose After Work
tasks.what: Intentionally designing a daily routine, social framework, and sense of purpose to support mental and physical health in retirement.
tasks.why: Loss of structure, identity, and social connection is one of the leading causes of depression and cognitive decline in early retirement — planning for this is as important as financial planning.
steps: Before your last day, articulate what you're retiring to, not just what you're retiring from — list activities, roles, or pursuits that will give your days meaning.
Create a weekly rhythm: schedule consistent wake times, physical activity, social commitments, and creative or intellectual pursuits to replace the structure a job naturally provided.
Maintain or build social connections deliberately: isolation is a major health risk for retirees. Join clubs, volunteer, take classes, or establish regular meetups with friends.
Consider phased retirement or part-time consulting if full retirement feels abrupt — many retirees find that a gradual transition improves wellbeing and extends financial runway.
Establish a volunteer role or encore career within the first 6 months of retirement; research shows purposeful engagement is the strongest predictor of retirement satisfaction.

Set learning goals: sign up for a class, online course, or skill you've always wanted to develop. Programs like OLLI (Osher Lifelong Learning Institutes) offer low-cost intellectual engagement at universities nationwide.

Discuss expectations with your spouse or partner — retirement changes the household dynamic significantly; research shows marital conflict spikes in the first year of retirement without proactive communication.
Schedule a 6-month and 12-month self-check-in to evaluate how you're feeling about retirement and what adjustments, if any, you want to make.

terms.def: A gradual transition from full-time work to full retirement, often involving reduced hours, consulting, or part-time roles over a period of months or years.
A second career pursued in retirement — often in a new field or nonprofit sector — that provides purpose, social connection, and sometimes income.

tasks.contacts: AARP retirement resources • aarp.org • 888-687-2277 • Osher Lifelong Learning Institutes (OLLI) • osanetwork.org • Encore.org for encore career resources
mistakes: Treating retirement as an extended vacation without any structure, then finding the lack of routine, purpose, and social interaction deeply unsatisfying after a few months.
Assuming a spouse will be a sufficient source of social connection — both partners need independent friendships and activities to avoid putting undue pressure on the relationship.

tasks.title: Planning for Long-Term Care
tasks.what: Preparing financially and logistically for the possibility of needing extended assistance with daily living — at home, in assisted living, or in a nursing facility.
tasks.why: About 70% of Americans over 65 will need some form of long-term care; the median cost of a private nursing home room was $108,405/year in 2024, and Medicare does not pay for custodial care.
steps: Understand what Medicare does not cover: Medicare pays for short-term skilled nursing care after a 3-day hospital stay (up to 100 days), but does not cover ongoing custodial care — help with bathing, dressing, and eating.

Understand what Medicaid does cover: Medicaid pays for long-term care but only after you've spent down most of your assets (typically to $2,000 for an individual). Medicaid eligibility rules and covered services vary by state.

Consider traditional long-term care insurance if you're in your mid-50s to mid-60s: premiums are significantly lower when you're younger and in good health. After 70, it becomes difficult to qualify and premiums are very high.

Evaluate hybrid long-term care policies: life insurance or annuity products with a long-term care rider that return premiums if care is never needed, addressing the "use it or lose it" concern of traditional LTC insurance.
If self-insuring (not buying LTC insurance), target reserving $300,000–$500,000 per person in liquid assets specifically for potential long-term care needs.
Discuss your preferences now: do you want to age in place, move to a continuing care retirement community (CCRC), or be near family? Knowing your preferences helps your family honor them.
Research aging-in-place modifications: grab bars, walk-in showers, stair lifts, and smart home technology can significantly delay or eliminate the need for facility care — at a fraction of the cost.
Include long-term care preferences in your advance healthcare directive and discuss them with your healthcare proxy so they know your wishes.

terms.def: Assistance with activities of daily living (bathing, dressing, eating, mobility) that is not medical in nature; not covered by Medicare.
Insurance that covers custodial care costs in a nursing home, assisted living facility, or at home, typically triggered when you need help with 2 or more activities of daily living.
A life insurance or annuity policy with a long-term care benefit rider; unused care benefits are returned as a death benefit, eliminating the use-it-or-lose-it concern.
A residential community that provides a continuum of care — independent living, assisted living, and skilled nursing — under one roof, often with an entrance fee plus monthly charges.

tasks.contacts: AARP Long-Term Care Information • aarp.org/caregiving • 888-687-2277 • American Association for Long-Term Care Insurance • aaltci.org • Genworth Cost of Care Survey • genworth.com/aging-and-you/finances/cost-of-care.html
mistakes: Assuming Medicare will cover nursing home or in-home custodial care — it will not, and this misunderstanding leaves many families financially unprepared.
Waiting until your late 60s or 70s to apply for long-term care insurance, when premiums are prohibitively expensive and health conditions may make you uninsurable.
Not discussing care preferences with family in advance, leading to conflict and costly decisions made in a crisis.
